title: Thermal state and concentration of gas hydrate and free gas of Coyhaique, Chilean Margin (44°30′S)
abstract: Detailed velocity analysis using pre-stack depth migration was performed on multichannel seismic reflection line RC2901-734 offshore Coyhaique, Chile (44°30′S). The velocity model shows a hydrate-bearing layer above the BSR with high velocity (1700–2200 m/s) and maximum thickness of 250 m, and a free gas-bearing layer below with low velocity (1250–1400 m/s). Geothermal gradient estimates range from 35 to 95 °C/km.

location.description: Offshore Coyhaique, southern Chile at ~44°30'S; gas hydrate and BSR study on named seismic line

data.name: R/V Conrad Line RC2901-734 — Coyhaique Offshore 44.5°S
data.description: Named MCS line RC2901-734 from R/V Conrad cruise offshore Coyhaique (44.5°S); analyzed with pre-stack depth migration for BSR velocity analysis, revealing gas hydrate layer (1700–2200 m/s) and free gas zone (1250–1400 m/s). [R/V Conrad RC2901 (Lamont, early 1990s) — no confirmed public data portal found]

_data_note: RC2901 cruise data accessible via MGDS. Line RC2901-734 is a named seismic profile from the R/V Conrad 1990 cruise.

processing.summary: Pre-stack depth migration (PSDM) was applied to the RC2901-734 MCS line offshore Coyhaique (44.5°S) to derive a detailed velocity model for gas hydrate and free gas quantification. The velocity model shows a high-velocity hydrate layer (1700–2200 m/s, up to 250 m thick) above the BSR and a low-velocity free gas zone (1250–1400 m/s) below. Geothermal gradients of 35–95°C/km are estimated from BSR depth.
workflow: 1. Standard processing using Seismic Unix (SU): stacking velocity analysis every 100 CDP (625 m)
2. Band-pass filter 15–70 Hz applied pre- and post-stack
3. Post-stack time migration (phase-shift method) for BSR identification
4. Selection of 20 km window (CDP 4000–7000) where BSR is strongest
5. Kirchhoff PreSDM with layer-stripping velocity model building (CIG r-parameter semblance)
6. Grid spacing 10 m (horizontal) × 12.5 m (vertical); initial velocity 1480 m/s
7. Iterative velocity updates per layer: seafloor (5 iter), layer 1 (28 iter), layer 2 to BSR (30 iter), free gas zone to BGR (26 iter)
8. Velocity gradient below BGR; final velocity model smoothed before final PreSDM
9. CIG stack to max 2500 m offset; trace mixing and band-pass filter on final stack
10. ±5% velocity perturbation sensitivity test; average depth error ~2%
11. BSR-derived geothermal gradient from BSR depth, seafloor depth, and seafloor temperature (2.2°C)
12. Gas hydrate/free gas concentration estimation via modified Domenico approach (Tinivella 1999/2002)
13. Sensitivity analysis on concentration estimates with ±5% velocity perturbation
processing.notes: PreSDM (Kirchhoff) with layer-stripping applied to a 20 km window of RC2901-734 where BSR is strongest; geothermal gradient derived from BSR depth; velocity accuracy confirmed by ±5% perturbation test

analysis_notes: PDF confirmed. Corrections applied: year_acquired 1990 → 1988 (RC2901 cruise was Jan–Feb 1988 per PDF); vessel name corrected to R/V Conrad RC2901; streamer_length_m=3000, channel_count=240, group_spacing_m=12.5, source_volume_cui=3742 (61.34 L), frequency_range_hz=[15,70] all added; software corrected from 'Echos (OGS)' to 'Seismic Unix (SU)'; migration_type corrected from PSSDM to PreSDM; workflow expanded to 13 steps. Data repository confirmed as LDEO (acknowledged). open_access_url present in JSON — plausibility unverified but retained.
